content:
Currently, you cannot (unless you manually use a different allocator etc.). How to handle this is part of the alloc topic which is going on (alongside making all allocations fallible etc.). We discussed it on Monday where we reached consensus on the approach to take, so we will have a solution soon!

I missed the discussion on Monday. Is there any documentations or articles about the discussion result?
No, sorry, but the result was that we will have our own custom alloc in-tree while trying hard to avoid diverging from upstream alloc.
The other two main options were going with a fully custom alloc (i.e. allowing any divergence), and going with upstream alloc (i.e. not even in-tree).
(plus using extension traits for missing functionality)

(plus using extension traits for missing functionality)

The opted option sounds like a tradeoff between the two others. :)
Yeah, it is the middle ground indeed :)

We need to be in-tree because 1) we need anyway to use some features like alloc does for e.g. a custom Arc, 2) we want to have freedom (at least in the beginning) to be able to customize as needed and 3) those customizations should be fairly easy to iterate on, without having to wait for the Rust side to implement them.

On the other hand, we do not want to diverge too much because 1) we would need to maintain it ourselves, 2) we want to reuse it (ideally as-is, eventually), 3) alloc currently uses quite a few compiler features that are not going to become stable soon or ever (but ideally we would want it to be a "normal" crate).

So the idea is having a middle-ground where we do customize alloc, but trying hard to keep it close enough to upstream so that eventually alloc can gain the features/customization points that we need; plus we can more easily synchronize bugfixes etc. from upstream.
This topic was moved here from #Help > Newbie Corner by Miguel Ojeda
I will document the decision etc. in Doc/rust.
I will even add a check to @ksquirrel to warn us about any changes to alloc so that people is conscious about it ;)

Miguel Ojeda said:

So the idea is having a middle-ground where we do customize alloc, but trying hard to keep it close enough to upstream so that eventually alloc can gain the features/customization points that we need; plus we can more easily synchronize bugfixes etc. from upstream.

Yeah, I fully agree with you.

Kernel is special but not Rust. Keeping consistence with upstream will ease the study curve for folks who want to join.

From the code I understand that the allocator currently uses kmalloc API, which means we will need a custom allocator in order to use vmalloc. This might be a dumb question, how can we tell vec! or something else to use vmalloc allocator (/once it is available/)? How the same driver can use both vmalloc and kmalloc APIs?

Santosh Sivaraj said:

From the code I understand that the allocator currently uses kmalloc API, which means we will need a custom allocator in order to use vmalloc.

That is right (unless we decide to add some new methods instead of using an allocator -- but we will see...).

Santosh Sivaraj said:

This might be a dumb question, how can we tell vec! or something else to use vmalloc allocator (/once it is available/)? How the same driver can use both vmalloc and kmalloc APIs?

So we will have several allocators to use as needed, which you can mix (i.e. one different allocator per instance of each data structure -- that is feature(allocator_api), similar as the C++ STL approach, i.e. you parametrize the type).

For macros like vec!, we can either 1) have custom versions, 2) or we modify the macros to allow to pass the allocator, 3) or we do not use them (I'd say in most cases they are not needed; and where needed, one can workaround it).

2) or we modify the macros to allow to pass the allocator

That would be a great little thing to work on upstream (that is less politically fraught than e.g. stabilizing the no-oom-panicking affordances we have. There might be roadblocks like marking macros unstable, but even if we get blocked on those it would still be good to know about them.
Is the plan to try to upstream all the new try_ methods we added?
Yeah
Actually, we already started! https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/86755
(well, that PR is not one of the new methods, but... :-)
However, it is fine if upstream wants to have e.g. different interfaces -- we can adapt later on our side
@Alex Gaynor Moved topic to #Library
For progress regarding upstreaming, I keep track at: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/408

Should we make a kernel::allocator based on alloc::allocator? GFP_XXX flags are peculiar to the kernel, and some binding functions accept this as a parameter. However, the alloc::alocator has no method to obtain this flag directly.

I suggest building a supertrait of alloc::allocator, and add some functions to it for kernel specific tasks.

@Miguel Ojeda your message in https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3271#issuecomment-1263919736 somewhat caught me by surprise. Agreed Rust for Linux is not going to commit to not needing a fork because of that RFC, but it never the less gets us a good deal closer?

It's great to see that that things are converging, and this is definitely something we do want.

Part of the reason we have the fork is to allow us (rust-for-linux) to experiment without having to wait for upstream Rust. We are not done with the experimentation: in particular, we need to settle on how we're going to handle the different types of allocations needed in the kernel (e.g., the gfp_t flags to kmalloc).

One option suggested by a kernel developer was to infer the allocation type from context (which would allow us to keep most Rust interfaces as they are), but that was shot down by Torvalds, so we may need some interface changes. I believe Miguel was referring to uncertainties of this kind when he said we don't know how we'll use liballoc in the future.

IOW, I'm afraid we may still have a little bit of extra divergence before we fully converge. But once we get there, we'd be more than happy to do away with this fork.
@Wedson Almeida Filho yeah keeping the ability to experiment makes perfect sense to me

I am just worried that what @Miguel Ojeda wrote could be taken as "oh, converging the code isn't even important because we will keep on experimenting anyways". I don't think that is what was meant, but just hoping to clear that up completely.

but it never the less gets us a good deal closer?

Yes, and I say that in the message myself, so I am not sure what you mean.

"oh, converging the code isn't even important because we will keep on experimenting anyways"

I am not sure why you think it can be read that way. In fact, I will clarify in the GitHub issue that the fact that we are trying to converge is even written in a file, not just a commit message.

However, what I want to make clear is that "converging the code" is not the primary goal here. The primary goal is to satisfy the kernel's needs, and if we can do that with alloc, then we are more than happy to drop it. In fact, maintaining alloc is not fun at all! But if we need to do something we cannot with alloc, then we will have to see what we need to do, even if that means not converging.
@Miguel Ojeda Yeah I think this is basically a case of conways law and "one servent too masters" a problem.
To the kernel devs, we have to make clear that the kernel comes first, nothing is so holy as not now allow to be changed.
To the Rust devs, we want to make clear that all forking is undesirable, extra maintainence burden, makes upgrading the compiler harder, etc.
There is no contradiction there, but it is a very different emphasis, basically as we are being the intermediary with two groups that aren't predisposed to care about each other.
Thanks @Miguel Ojeda! Very much appreciate that last reply :)

John Ericson said:

two groups that aren't predisposed to care about each other.

I think it is important to change the perception that the two groups have to be antagonistic :)

I apologize if the replies in the RFC appeared to be a bit harsh, but to accomplish the above sometimes I feel it is needed to make sure we don't get misunderstandings from both sides.
@Miguel Ojeda is been a few months, but I had some back and forth on Zulip today and yesterday and I think we have a way forward with the lib team's tentative approval!

I don't know how to link an individual message from the Zulip phone app, but see the bottom of this topic https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/197181-t-libs.2Fwg-allocators/topic/Split.20or.20merge.20std.2Falloc.2Fcore.3F
@bjorn3 pinged me about it a few hours ago -- thanks @John Ericson for all your work around this over the years :)

Just curious, are the things on https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/408 the only reasons to maintain a seperate alloc crate? Because if try_ methods are all that's left, I think that can be handled with the standard alloc crate plus extension traits (like https://github.com/microsoft/rust_fallible_vec)
That doesn't prevent accidentally using a non-fallible version of a method.
Ah, didn't realize that was part of it
